label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this problem step by step. It's a bit complex, but I'll take it slowly. First, let me restate the problem to make sure I understand it. There's a teacher with four students. The teacher picks four consecutive two-digit integers, forming set S. Each student gets one of these numbers. The teacher tells the class that the numbers are four consecutive two-digit integers, that one of them is divisible by 6, and another is divisible by 7 (different numbers). Then the teacher asks if anyone can figure out what S is. All four students say "no" in unison. But then, after hearing that all four said "no," each student can figure out S. We need to find the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. Alright, so the key points here are: 1. Four consecutive two-digit integers. Let's denote them as n, n+1, n+2, n+3. So n is between 10 and 97 (since n+3 must be a two-digit number, so n ≤ 97). 2. One number is divisible by 6, another by 7. These are different numbers, so there's at least one multiple of 6 and one multiple of 7 in the four numbers. 3. All four students initially say they can't determine S. Then, after knowing that everyone couldn't determine it, they can. So we need to find such sets S where this is possible. First, let me think about the initial information. Each student has one number from S. The students know that the numbers are four consecutive two-digit integers, one is divisible by 6, another by 7. Each student knows their own number but not the others. When the teacher asks if they can deduce S, all say no. Then, based on that, they can figure it out. This is similar to some logic puzzles where knowledge of others' knowledge leads to conclusions. So, first, for each possible set S (four consecutive numbers, with one multiple of 6 and one multiple of 7), we need to determine if there's a number in S such that knowing only that number, and knowing the other conditions, you can uniquely determine S. If there is such a number, then the student with that number would have said "yes," but since all said "no," we can eliminate all sets where any number in S would allow unique determination.
